questions.question: Order the steps of the Faster R-CNN detection pipeline from first to last.
items: Apply non-maximum suppression to remove duplicate detections
Generate region proposals using the RPN
Classify each proposal and refine its bounding box
Extract fixed-size features for each proposal with ROI Pooling
Pass the image through the backbone CNN to get feature maps
correct_order: Pass the image through the backbone CNN to get feature maps
Generate region proposals using the RPN
Extract fixed-size features for each proposal with ROI Pooling
Classify each proposal and refine its bounding box
Apply non-maximum suppression to remove duplicate detections
questions.explanation: Faster R-CNN works in stages: the backbone extracts features, the RPN proposes candidate regions, ROI Pooling standardizes each proposal's features, classification and regression heads make predictions, and NMS removes duplicate detections at the end.

questions.question: Order the steps of non-maximum suppression (NMS) from first to last.
items: Repeat until no detections remain
Remove all detections that overlap too much with the selected one
Pick the detection with the highest confidence
Add the selected detection to the final output
Sort all detections by confidence score
correct_order: Sort all detections by confidence score
Pick the detection with the highest confidence
Add the selected detection to the final output
Remove all detections that overlap too much with the selected one
Repeat until no detections remain
questions.explanation: NMS works greedily: sort by confidence, take the top detection, add it to the output, suppress all overlapping boxes (those with IoU above the threshold), and repeat until all detections have been processed or removed.

questions.question: Order how a Vision Transformer (ViT) processes an image from input to output.
items: Process the sequence through Transformer encoder layers
Use the [CLS] token output for classification
Split the image into fixed-size patches
Embed each patch as a vector and add position embeddings
Prepend a learnable [CLS] token to the sequence
correct_order: Split the image into fixed-size patches
Embed each patch as a vector and add position embeddings
Prepend a learnable [CLS] token to the sequence
Process the sequence through Transformer encoder layers
Use the [CLS] token output for classification
questions.explanation: ViT first divides the image into patches, embeds each patch into a vector and adds position information, prepends a special [CLS] token, processes everything through Transformer layers with self-attention, and finally uses the [CLS] token's output for classification.

questions.question: Order the steps of transfer learning for a new image classification task.
items: Evaluate the model on the test set
Load a pretrained model (e.g., ResNet trained on ImageNet)
Fine-tune the model on the target dataset
Prepare and augment the target dataset
Replace the classification head to match the new number of classes
correct_order: Load a pretrained model (e.g., ResNet trained on ImageNet)
Replace the classification head to match the new number of classes
Prepare and augment the target dataset
Fine-tune the model on the target dataset
Evaluate the model on the test set
questions.explanation: Transfer learning starts by loading a pretrained model, then replacing the final layer to match the target classes. Next, you prepare your data with augmentation, fine-tune (with some or all layers frozen), and finally evaluate on a held-out test set.

questions.question: Order data flow through a U-Net from input to output.
items: Apply a final 1x1 convolution for per-pixel class prediction
Encoder downsamples and extracts features
Decoder upsamples to restore resolution
Feed the image into the network
Skip connections pass encoder features to the decoder
correct_order: Feed the image into the network
Encoder downsamples and extracts features
Decoder upsamples to restore resolution
Skip connections pass encoder features to the decoder
Apply a final 1x1 convolution for per-pixel class prediction
questions.explanation: U-Net takes an image, passes it through the encoder (which captures context by downsampling), then the decoder (which restores resolution by upsampling). Skip connections bridge encoder and decoder at each level to preserve spatial detail. A final 1x1 convolution maps features to class predictions per pixel.

questions.question: Order these computer vision tasks from least to most detailed output.
items: Instance segmentation
Object detection
Semantic segmentation
Image classification
correct_order: Image classification
Object detection
Semantic segmentation
Instance segmentation
questions.explanation: Classification gives one label per image (least detail). Detection adds bounding boxes to localize objects. Semantic segmentation labels every pixel by class. Instance segmentation provides the most detail by labeling every pixel and distinguishing individual objects of the same class.

questions.question: Order the steps for building a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN).
items: Smooth merged features with a convolution
Upsample top-level features and merge with lateral outputs
Use lateral connections to match channel dimensions
Start the top-down pathway from the deepest feature map
Build bottom-up features using the backbone CNN
correct_order: Build bottom-up features using the backbone CNN
Start the top-down pathway from the deepest feature map
Use lateral connections to match channel dimensions
Upsample top-level features and merge with lateral outputs
Smooth merged features with a convolution
questions.explanation: FPN first extracts features at multiple scales through the backbone (bottom-up). Then it starts from the deepest, most semantic features and works back up. Lateral connections adjust channel dimensions, upsampled features are merged with corresponding bottom-up features, and a final convolution smooths out the result.

questions.question: Order the gradual unfreezing fine-tuning strategy from first step to last.
items: Unfreeze middle layers and continue training with a lower learning rate
Unfreeze early layers and train with the lowest learning rate
Train only the new classification head with all other layers frozen
Load the pretrained model and replace the classification head
Evaluate the final model on the test set
correct_order: Load the pretrained model and replace the classification head
Train only the new classification head with all other layers frozen
Unfreeze middle layers and continue training with a lower learning rate
Unfreeze early layers and train with the lowest learning rate
Evaluate the final model on the test set
questions.explanation: Gradual unfreezing starts by training only the new head, then progressively unfreezes deeper layers with smaller learning rates. This prevents catastrophic forgetting by letting each layer adapt slowly while preserving pretrained knowledge in the earlier layers.

questions.question: Order the steps of generating a Class Activation Map (CAM) visualization.
items: Overlay the heatmap on the original image
Use the weights to create a weighted combination of feature maps
Run a forward pass to get the model's prediction and final feature maps
Upsample the result to match the input image size
Get the weights connecting the final feature maps to the predicted class
correct_order: Run a forward pass to get the model's prediction and final feature maps
Get the weights connecting the final feature maps to the predicted class
Use the weights to create a weighted combination of feature maps
Upsample the result to match the input image size
Overlay the heatmap on the original image
questions.explanation: CAM generation starts with a forward pass to obtain features and a prediction. The weights linking final features to the predicted class indicate each feature map's importance. These weights produce a weighted sum of feature maps, which is then upsampled and overlaid on the original image as a heatmap.

questions.question: Order the object detection process from raw image to final detections.
items: Apply non-maximum suppression to remove duplicates
Output final bounding boxes with class labels and confidence scores
Extract features from the image using a CNN backbone
Generate candidate bounding boxes (via anchors or grid)
Score and refine each candidate box
correct_order: Extract features from the image using a CNN backbone
Generate candidate bounding boxes (via anchors or grid)
Score and refine each candidate box
Apply non-maximum suppression to remove duplicates
Output final bounding boxes with class labels and confidence scores
questions.explanation: Object detection starts by extracting image features, then generating candidate boxes (through anchors in Faster R-CNN or a grid in YOLO). Each candidate is scored and refined. NMS removes redundant overlapping detections. The surviving boxes with their class labels and confidence scores are the final output.
